subject: ap-chemistry
title: Electrochemical Cells & Cell Potential
stimulus:
A student is given a standard galvanic cell, represented above, that has a Cu electrode and a Sn electrode.
As current flows through the cell, the student determines that the Cu electrode increases in mass and the
Sn electrode decreases in mass.

parts.prompt:
Identify the electrode at which oxidation is occurring. Explain your reasoning based on the student's
observations.
parts.scoring_notes: The Sn electrode is the anode (oxidation). It decreases in mass because Sn atoms are oxidized to Sn$^{2+}$(aq) ions and enter solution. The Cu electrode increases in mass because Cu$^{2+}$(aq) is reduced to Cu(s) and deposits onto the electrode.

scoring_points.description: For identifying the Sn electrode as the site of oxidation with correct reasoning: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Since the Sn electrode is losing mass, Sn atoms must be forming Sn2+(aq). This process is oxidation. OR because the cell operates, E° must be positive and, based on the E° values from the table, it must be Sn that is oxidized. 1 point is earned for the correct answer with justification.

sample_responses.response: Oxidation occurs at the Sn electrode. The observation that the Sn electrode decreases in mass shows that Sn atoms are being converted to $Sn^{2+}(aq)$ ions and dissolving into solution — this is oxidation (loss of electrons).
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the Sn electrode and ties the mass decrease to oxidation of Sn metal to Sn²⁺ ions.

sample_responses.response: Oxidation occurs at the Cu electrode because Cu is being deposited there.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. Cu is being reduced (gaining electrons) at the cathode; its mass increases by deposition. Oxidation (loss of electrons, mass decrease) occurs at the Sn anode.

required_elements: The Sn electrode decreases in mass, indicating Sn atoms are oxidized to Sn$^{2+}$ ions and dissolve into solution.
alternatives.correct_example: Oxidation occurs at the Sn electrode. The observation that the Sn electrode decreases in mass is consistent with Sn being oxidized: Sn(s) → Sn$^{2+}$(aq) + 2 e$^-$. The Sn atoms lose mass as they enter the solution as ions.

wrong_examples: Saying the Cu electrode — Cu is increasing in mass, which is characteristic of reduction (Cu$^{2+}$ depositing), not oxidation.
common_traps: Oxidation = loss of electrons = the anode = electrode that decreases in mass as metal atoms dissolve into solution.

parts.prompt: As the mass of the Sn electrode decreases, where does the mass go?
parts.scoring_notes: The mass goes into the solution as Sn$^{2+}$(aq) ions. Sn atoms are oxidized at the anode and dissolve into the Sn(NO$_3$)$_2$ solution.

scoring_points.description: For stating mass goes into solution as Sn$^{2+}$ ions: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The atoms on the Sn electrode are going into the solution as Sn2+ ions. 1 point is earned for the correct answer.

sample_responses.response: The mass goes into the solution as $Sn^{2+}(aq)$ ions. Each Sn atom is oxidized ($Sn \rightarrow Sn^{2+} + 2e^-$) and dissolves into the $Sn(NO_3)_2$ solution in the anode compartment.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly states that the Sn atoms become aqueous Sn²⁺ ions, consistent with the oxidation half-reaction at the anode.

sample_responses.response: The mass moves through the wire to the Cu electrode where it is deposited.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. Electrons travel through the wire, not mass. The Sn mass enters the solution as $Sn^{2+}$ ions; it does not travel to the Cu electrode.

required_elements: Mass goes into the solution as Sn$^{2+}$(aq) ions.
alternatives.correct_example: The Sn atoms on the electrode are oxidized (Sn → Sn$^{2+}$ + 2 e$^-$) and the resulting Sn$^{2+}$ ions dissolve into the surrounding Sn(NO$_3$)$_2$ solution, increasing [Sn$^{2+}$] in the anode compartment.

wrong_examples: Saying mass moves to the other electrode — metal ions travel through solution, not across the electrodes directly.
Saying mass disappears — conservation of mass; atoms become ions in solution.
common_traps: Solid metal → dissolved metal ions: the mass leaves the electrode and enters the solution.

parts.prompt:
In the expanded view of the center portion of the salt bridge shown in the diagram below, draw and label a
particle view of what occurs in the salt bridge as the cell begins to operate. Omit solvent molecules and use
arrows to show the movement of particles.
parts.scoring_notes: Cations in the salt bridge migrate toward the cathode compartment (Cu side); anions migrate toward the anode compartment (Sn side). Arrows must show cations moving right (toward cathode) and anions moving left (toward anode).

parts.prompt:
A nonstandard cell is made by replacing the 1.0 M solutions of $Cu(NO_3)_2$ and $Sn(NO_3)_2$ in the standard cell
with 0.50 M solutions of $Cu(NO_3)_2$ and $Sn(NO_3)_2$. The volumes of solutions in the nonstandard cell are
identical to those in the standard cell.
(i) Is the cell potential of the nonstandard cell greater than, less than, or equal to the cell potential of the
standard cell? Justify your answer.
(ii) Both the standard and nonstandard cells can be used to power an electronic device. Would the
nonstandard cell power the device for the same time, a longer time, or a shorter time as compared with
the standard cell? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (d)(i): Equal to, or slightly different — the Q expression for the overall cell has [Cu$^{2+}$] in the numerator and [Sn$^{2+}$] in the denominator: Q = [Sn$^{2+}$]/[Cu$^{2+}$]. Halving both concentrations cancels: Q remains 1, so $E = E°$. Accept 'equal to' with Nernst equation reasoning. Part (d)(ii): Shorter time. With half the concentration of ions and the same volume, the nonstandard cell has half as many moles of reactants and will run out sooner.

scoring_points.description: For 'equal to' with correct Nernst/Q reasoning: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: It is the same. In the cell reaction Q = [Sn2+]/[Cu2+], and the concentrations of Sn2+ and Cu2+ are equal to each other in both cases. 1 point is earned for the correct answer with justification.

sample_responses.response: Equal to. $Q = [Sn^{2+}]/[Cu^{2+}] = 0.50/0.50 = 1$, the same as in the standard cell where both are 1.0 M ($Q = 1.0/1.0 = 1$). By the Nernst equation, $E = E° - (0.0592/n)\log Q = E°$ when $Q = 1$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly calculates Q for both conditions and shows Q = 1 in both, so E = E° (standard potential) in both cases.

sample_responses.response: Less than. Lower concentrations mean fewer ions, so less current and a lower cell potential.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. The cell potential depends on Q, not absolute concentration. Since both ion concentrations are halved equally, Q is unchanged and E = E°.

required_elements: Q = [Sn$^{2+}$]/[Cu$^{2+}$]. Halving both concentrations equally leaves Q = 1 (same as standard), so $E = E°$.
alternatives.correct_example: Equal to. For the overall cell reaction Sn(s) + Cu$^{2+}$(aq) → Sn$^{2+}$(aq) + Cu(s), $Q = [Sn^{2+}]/[Cu^{2+}]$. At 0.50 M each: Q = 0.50/0.50 = 1. From the Nernst equation, $E = E° - (0.0592/2)\log(1) = E°$. The potential is the same as the standard cell.

wrong_examples: Saying less than or greater than — the ratio [Sn$^{2+}$]/[Cu$^{2+}$] = 1 at equal concentrations regardless of their absolute values.
common_traps: The Nernst equation depends on Q, not absolute concentrations. When both ion concentrations change by the same factor, Q is unchanged.

scoring_points.description: For 'shorter time' with reasoning about fewer moles of reactants: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The nonstandard cell would power the device for a shorter time because the supply of Cu2+ ions will be exhausted more quickly. OR …because the reaction will reach E = 0 more quickly. 1 point is earned for the correct answer with justification.

sample_responses.response: Shorter time. With half the concentration of $Cu^{2+}$ in the same volume, the nonstandard cell has half as many moles of $Cu^{2+}$ available for reduction. Fewer moles of reactant means the cell will exhaust its supply sooner and stop producing current earlier.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly distinguishes between voltage (same) and capacity (fewer moles = shorter runtime), a key distinction in electrochemistry.

sample_responses.response: The same time. Since the cell potential is the same, the cell will run for the same duration.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. Cell potential tells you whether the cell can work; the moles of reactant determine how long it runs. Fewer moles of $Cu^{2+}$ means shorter runtime.

required_elements: The nonstandard cell contains fewer moles of Cu$^{2+}$ (half the concentration, same volume) and less Sn(s) in solution — fewer moles of reactants available.
alternatives.correct_example: Shorter time. The nonstandard cell has half the concentration of Cu$^{2+}$ in the same volume, meaning fewer total moles of Cu$^{2+}$ available for reduction. The cell will exhaust its reactants sooner and stop producing current earlier.

wrong_examples: Same time — the lower concentration means fewer moles of Cu$^{2+}$ to reduce, so the cell runs out faster.
common_traps: Cell potential (voltage) tells you if the cell can work; moles of reactants tell you how long it can work. Fewer moles → shorter runtime.

parts.prompt:
In another experiment, the student places a new Sn electrode into a fresh solution of 1.0 M $Cu(NO_3)_2$.
Half-Reaction E° (V)
Cu$^+$ + e$^-$ → Cu(s) 0.52
Cu$^{2+}$ + 2 e$^-$ → Cu(s) 0.34
Sn$^{4+}$ + 2 e$^-$ → Sn$^{2+}$ 0.15
Sn$^{2+}$ + 2 e$^-$ → Sn(s) −0.14
(i) Using information from the table above, write a net-ionic equation for the reaction between the
Sn electrode and the $Cu(NO_3)_2$ solution that would be thermodynamically favorable. Justify that
the reaction is thermodynamically favorable.
(ii) Calculate the value of ΔG° for the reaction. Include units with your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: The most favorable reaction pairs the most positive cathode with the most negative anode. Reduction of Cu$^{2+}$ (E° = +0.34 V) and oxidation of Sn (E° = +0.14 V anode, reversed): $E°_{cell}$ = 0.34 − (−0.14) = 0.48 V. Net: Sn(s) + Cu$^{2+}$(aq) → Sn$^{2+}$(aq) + Cu(s). ΔG° = −nFE° = −(2)(96485)(0.48) = −92,600 J/mol ≈ −93 kJ/mol.

scoring_points.description: For the correct net-ionic equation: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Cu2+(aq) + Sn(s) → Cu(s) + Sn2+(aq). E° is positive (0.34 V + 0.14 V = 0.48 V), therefore the reaction is thermodynamically favorable. 1 point for the correct net-ionic equation; 1 point for a correct justification.

sample_responses.response:
$Cu^{2+}(aq) + Sn(s) \rightarrow Cu(s) + Sn^{2+}(aq)$
$E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode} = 0.34\text{ V} - (-0.14\text{ V}) = +0.48\text{ V} > 0$, so the reaction is thermodynamically favorable.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly writes the net-ionic equation with Sn oxidized and Cu²⁺ reduced, and uses the positive E°cell to confirm thermodynamic favorability.
This is the reverse (non-spontaneous) reaction. The spontaneous direction has Sn oxidized and Cu²⁺ reduced, giving E°cell = +0.48 V > 0.

alternatives.correct_example: Sn(s) + Cu$^{2+}$(aq) → Sn$^{2+}$(aq) + Cu(s). Sn is oxidized (anode reaction) and Cu$^{2+}$ is reduced (cathode reaction). This is thermodynamically favorable because $E°_{cell} > 0$.

wrong_examples: Including spectator ions NO$_3^-$ — they are spectators and should not appear.
Reversing the reaction — the reaction must proceed with positive $E°_{cell}$ to be thermodynamically favorable.
common_traps: The spontaneous direction is determined by which reaction gives a positive $E°_{cell}$. Sn is oxidized (E° = −0.14 V reversed → +0.14 V) and Cu$^{2+}$ is reduced (E° = +0.34 V).

scoring_points.description: For justifying thermodynamic favorability with $E°_{cell}$ > 0: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: E° is positive (0.34 V + 0.14 V = 0.48 V), therefore the reaction is thermodynamically favorable. 1 point is earned for a correct justification (unit not needed in calculation).

sample_responses.response: $E°_{cell} = 0.34\text{ V} - (-0.14\text{ V}) = +0.48\text{ V}$. Since $E°_{cell} > 0$, $\Delta G° < 0$ and the reaction is thermodynamically spontaneous (favorable).
sample_responses.commentary: Correct calculation of $E°_{cell}$ using $E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode}$ and correct inference that positive $E°$ means thermodynamically favorable.

sample_responses.response: $E°_{cell} = 0.34 + (-0.14) = +0.20\text{ V}$, so the reaction is favorable.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect arithmetic. The anode $E°$ is $-0.14$ V, but when calculating $E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode}$, it is $0.34 - (-0.14) = +0.48$ V, not $0.34 + (-0.14) = +0.20$ V.

required_elements: $E°_{cell} > 0$ indicates the reaction is thermodynamically spontaneous.
alternatives.correct_example: $E°_{cell} = 0.34\text{ V} - (-0.14\text{ V}) = +0.48\text{ V}$. A positive $E°_{cell}$ means $\Delta G° < 0$, confirming the reaction is thermodynamically favorable (spontaneous).

wrong_examples: Not computing $E°_{cell}$ — must show the calculation and that the result is positive.
common_traps: $E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode}$. Positive $E°_{cell}$ → spontaneous. Do not add; subtract the anode potential.

scoring_points.description: For the correct ΔG° with units: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: ΔG° = −nFE° = −(2 mol e−)(96,485 C/mol)(0.48 J/C) = −93,000 J/mol = −93 kJ/mol. 1 point for correct n; 1 point for correct answer with unit.

sample_responses.response: $\Delta G° = -nFE° = -(2\text{ mol } e^-)(96485\text{ C/mol})(0.48\text{ V}) = -92626\text{ J/mol} \approx -93\text{ kJ/mol}$
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies n = 2 (two electrons transferred in the balanced equation), uses the Faraday constant, and reports the answer in kJ/mol.
Incorrect: n = 1 is wrong. The balanced reaction $Cu^{2+} + Sn \rightarrow Cu + Sn^{2+}$ involves transfer of 2 electrons per formula unit, so n = 2.

alternatives.correct_example:
$\Delta G° = -nFE° = -(2\text{ mol e}^-)(96485\text{ C/mol})(0.48\text{ V}) = -92600\text{ J/mol} = -93\text{ kJ/mol}$
$n = 2$ electrons transferred (balanced half-reactions each involve 2 e$^-$).

wrong_examples: Reporting a positive ΔG° — the negative sign in $\Delta G° = -nFE°$ is essential.
common_traps: $n$ is the number of electrons transferred in the balanced equation. For this Sn/Cu$^{2+}$ reaction, $n = 2$.
